source: Self-Execution Simulation Improves Coding Models (Maimon et al.)
summary: Coding LLMs are trained to simulate program execution step-by-step as inner monologue (verbal execution traces), enabling self-verification of multiple candidate solutions and iterative self-fixing by predicting test outcomes before running them. Achieves significant gains on competitive programming benchmarks.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — maps directly to the gap between Implementing and AIReview; a simulation-based pre-review gate could catch bugs cheaply before an expensive Codex review cycle.

summary: Open event protocol for agent-to-UI communication, defining typed events (step:started, step:completed, tool:call, file:edited) that frontends render as a structured live activity feed rather than opaque text streams.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — AgentForge already has raw process:output SSE events but no semantic step-level typed events; implementing the protocol addresses limitation #9 (no streaming/incremental output).

summary: Minimal self-improving experiment loop where an agent edits training scripts, runs time-boxed experiments, measures improvement, keeps or discards changes, and repeats—autonomously. Ran 700 experiments and discovered 20 optimizations over two days. 66k GitHub stars.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — core decomposition principle (too-large tasks must be split before execution) and experiment-loop pattern inspire plan-complexity gating. Also a candidate for applying the loop to orchestration config self-improvement.

summary: Agentic orchestrator for parallel coding agents (Claude Code, Codex, Aider) that plans tasks, spawns agents into isolated git worktrees with their own PRs, automatically fixes CI failures and addresses review comments. Agent-agnostic, runtime-agnostic, tracker-agnostic (Linear support included).
relevanceToAgentForge: High — live streaming of agent console output and per-agent isolation inspired HID-103 (streaming) and HID-104 (sub-issue decomposition). Architecture closely mirrors AgentForge's goals.

source: MAGMA: Multi-Graph based Agentic Memory Architecture (FredJiang0324 et al.)
summary: Memory system that indexes each memory across semantic, temporal, causal, and entity graphs simultaneously. Achieves 0.7 LoCoMo judge score (outperforming MemoryOS, A-MEM, Nemori by 18-45%). 95% token reduction vs full-context, 40% faster retrieval than next-best baseline.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — existing issues HID-84, HID-86, HID-87, HID-92 cover various forms of cross-run memory; MAGMA's graph-based multi-dimensional retrieval is architecturally distinct but would overlap significantly with those issues in scope.

summary: Multi-agent system (planner + multiple workers) that incorporates self-assessment and self-feedback for long-horizon coding tasks, enabling approach-level corrections beyond local failure handling. Published December 2025.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — approach-level correction from HID-81 (reflection-driven plan repair) covers similar ground; PARC's multi-worker parallelism pattern is interesting but not immediately applicable given single-agent-per-stage constraint.

summary: Survey of agent memory architectures in 2026: three-tier taxonomy (episodic, semantic, procedural). Production trend toward hybrid retrieval (semantic similarity + keyword + entity matching fused in parallel). MAGMA leads benchmarks.
relevanceToAgentForge: Low — useful context for memory architecture decisions; specific patterns largely covered by existing issues.

summary: Agents predict and pre-execute likely future tool calls during LLM thinking time (analogous to CPU branch prediction), using idle time between tool calls to speculatively pre-plan next steps.
relevanceToAgentForge: Low — HID-101 already covers speculative implementation skeleton during plan-review idle window, which is the direct AgentForge analog.

summary: Circuit breaker pattern for AI agents: three states (Closed/Open/Half-Open), automatic traffic redirection on repeated failures, prevents retry storms. Practical implementation guide with threshold examples.
relevanceToAgentForge: Low — already covered by multiple existing issues: HID-21, HID-33, HID-38, HID-51, HID-63, HID-66, HID-82, HID-90, HID-96.

summary: Karpathy's framing of the 'agentic engineering era' where humans direct and orchestrate agents rather than writing code. Recursive agentic loops for continuous self-improvement. Joined Anthropic in early 2026.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — conceptual inspiration for long-horizon agent orchestration; the decomposition principle applied to HID-104.

source: BAGEN: Doomed Trajectory Early Abort (Northwestern, Zihan Wang et al.)
summary: LLM agents are systematically over-optimistic; BAGEN detects doomed trajectories early via budget-aware analysis and aborts to save tokens.
relevanceToAgentForge: Low — already covered by HID-100 (mid-run tool-call budget tracker with doomed-trajectory early abort).

source: Tree of Thoughts multi-branch reasoning for agent planning (Shunyu Yao, blog.mean.ceo)
summary: 2026 guide on applying ToT with beam search, heuristic scoring, and pruning to LLM agent planning; enables testing multiple plan paths and cutting weak options early.
relevanceToAgentForge: Low — HID-60 (multi-perspective plan drafting with consensus merge) already covers multi-plan generation and selection.

summary: Full OpenTelemetry v1.41 support for LangGraph as of March 2026, adding agent/workflow/tool/model spans with required latency and token-usage metrics. Native framework integrations for structured tracing.
relevanceToAgentForge: Low — AgentForge uses its own telemetry (HID-83, HID-52, HID-96) and is not based on LangGraph; OpenTelemetry export would be a useful but low-priority addition.
